sentences.text: We went back in 1944 after D-Day.

Very briefly, after Dunkirk and after the collapse of France and after France gave in and started collaborating with the Germans, it was patently clear that the old British Empire was on its own as far as the field was concerned.

And although many Frenchmen were evacuated from Dunkirk along with the British, they-- most of them opted to go back to France and in fact, they were conveyed back to France on British transports under the orders of Winston Churchill.
Now, I must make it clear that it wasn't the United Kingdom that stood alone, as is so often said.
It was the British Commonwealth of Nations.
And they were supported.
Because of the link between President Roosevelt of the States and Winston Churchill, we were given a lot of material help, which I might say we had to pay for.
It was cash and carry.
And when the cash ran out, President Roosevelt thought up the wonderful idea of lease-lend.
And then, of course, once the Japs attacked Pearl Harbor, they were in it.
But I must make the point that at no time was the United Kingdom on its own.
It was the British Commonwealth of Nations.
Now, after Dunkirk, the army had to be rebuilt.
The army came back from Dunkirk without any weapons, without any transport, and with such stuff as they could carry.
And that wasn't very much.
So we had to requip, we had to completely retrain.
Commanders who failed in France was sacked, new ones had to be found.
And in fact, I was mixed up in all that because I went to join a regiment which had never had any guns.
And so you worked day and night getting ready for what one saw as the invasion.
We know that the Germans had this plan because it was called Operation Sea Lion.
But it all depended on defeating the Royal Air Force.
And in the Battle of Britain, the Luftwaffe was well and truly trounced.
And then Hitler turned around and looked again towards his old enemy, the Soviet Union.

Now, as far as I was concerned, I joined a field regiment and I was part of the millions of men who were having to work day and night to become efficient and do our jobs and support the infantry or whatever it was.
We had to learn to fight on the beaches and we had to learn to fight in the streets and everywhere else and it was very busy time.
Now, in early 1942, I got the opportunity to move from a field regiment to a mountain regiment which was equipped with horses and mules.
And knowing that the commanding officer was my old battery commander, who I admired intensely, I seized you go back under his command.
If I was going to fight in England, then I wanted to fight under somebody I trusted because after all was said and done, it was my neck and my skin.
And so I went to mountain artillery in early May 19-- I think it was early May 19-- no, it would be round about Easter in 1942.
And I actually joined a battery which was stationed at Wheedon in Northamptonshire, where my wife was, in fact, serving in the women's forces next door and we got married in 1944.
And we've now been married well over 50 years.
Speaks for itself.
Well, we spent a lot of time-- the-- the powers that be decided they were going to get a force together to go into what I now know was to be Norway, but it was always called mountain and sub-Arctic.
And we trained like fury in the Scottish Highlands and it was very, very hard going.
And then the-- the raid took place and it was quite clear that there was going to be no invasion of the continent in 1942.
And eventually, as you-- all the world knows, D-Day took place on the 6th of June, 1944.

And there we were stuck up in Scotland with our horses and mules, training to go and land in Norway, where Hitler kept an old mountain army who were desperately needed by 1944, either on the Eastern Front against the Soviets or in Europe or in Yugoslavia chasing Tito.
We-- the invasion took place and of course, it was a resounding success.
And then somebody had the great idea of forming an Allied Air Army too land behind the fighting in Normandy and then to break out across France, the low countries, hopefully into Germany.

And overnight, they decided that they would convert the Mountain Division to an air-portable s So they gave us Jeeps and trailers and we left our horses and mules beyond and we became mechanized overnight.
The First Mountain Regiment.
And it was a part of the 52nd Scottish Division.
Oh, he was Radford, Jock Radford, MC.
He was a horse [INAUDIBLE] man, a great man.
He had time for everybody under his command.
He never made you feel indifferent.
He could make you feel very angry with yourself if you had been a clot, you know what I mean?
He was incredibly efficient and he was very devout, very devout.
And he won a military cross in the First World War and we're talking bout the Second World War, so he was no spring chicken.
In fact, I remained very great friends until he died.
And oh, he was a super chap, super chap.
It wasn't renamed, it just continued.
They merely changed our form of draft from animal draft to mechanized air-portable.
But we're still called-- we were still equipped with the mountain guns.
Our guns were not the same as the field army.

You could take our guns to bits, which was a great asset because you could put them in the landing craft-- little landing craft-- in bits and pieces and then assemble them on the shore where you landed.
And we did that.
And it's a big shock to even a German soldier, and the Germans are good soldiers.
It was a shock to him to suddenly find that he had a 3.7 Howitzer firing at him over about 50 yards' range.
He didn't like it.
By that time-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --by that time, we knew that there was, in fact, an extermination program going on, although it was hotly denied by the Germans.
And of course, the very fact that they were denying it made us more convinced than ever it was true.
But we had no idea that we-- that they-- the program was such as it was.
Bear in mind that a British newspaper-- in fact, I've got one in that bookcase but I can't get it out here-- a British newspaper, if it consisted of four sheets, it was a pretty big newspaper then.
The newsprint was very heavily rationed.
And-- but we-- we knew that, you know, things were pretty bad.
But we didn't know they were as bad as they were.
Could we trust the news?
Well, I certainly trusted it.
The British news, the BBC.
The BBC, I believe, was the only news which was trustworthy.
The nine o'clock news used to be preceded by the three dots and a dash, the V-sign, da-da-da-da, da-da-da-da, da-da-da-da.
And people all over Europe risked their lives to listen to it.
That news was good because it was only a fragment mews.
Are you with me?
It was just a fragment of news, but it was-- it was truthful.
When we finished with-- when we finished the campaign on the [INAUDIBLE], we came to a small village called Vught in Holland-- V-U-G-H-T, Vught.
And then when we learned that there had been in Vught a concentration camp-- now, that was the first concentration camp which we ever heard about.
We didn't go into it, we didn't go near it.
But we heard there had been a concentration camp there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We knew that there had been evacuation of Jews because various people told us, oh, that house was owned by Jews that they've been taken away.
But we didn't know of it as a program.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the-- when we were going into Germany and we got halfway across Germany and we had a very big battle on the River Aller, we-- we had a-- it was a good old scrum.
But anyway, we won and we were actually-- although we didn't know it, we were actually heading towards Belsen concentration camp.
Now, that was the first time we really heard of these camps.
And at that time, the-- we were ready to break out on the road up towards this camp.
And I well remember some German officers coming in under the white flag.

And we sent them back and they were telling us-- well, they didn't tell me, but they told the infantry with the kommandos-- that there was a camp up the road where there was a lot of prisoners and typhus was rampant.
And we really-- they needed help to do something about it because the German Army couldn't cope with it.
And what happened was that they gave us a crossing farther upriver and we pulled out of the crossing and then the army [INAUDIBLE] behind us-- who it was, I don't know-- took over at Belsen.
And we continued our drive to the Elbe.
But I think that that was when-- was the first time.
Because there was a hell of a row that this sort of thing could have happened in a civilized society.
Because on the American front at the same time, Buchenwald was found.
In actual fact, one of my chaps who was a prisoner of war was found in Buchenwald.
And he's alive today, but by God, you can read newspapers through him he's still so thin-- metaphorically speaking.

So I would say that it would be in the early weeks of April, about the second week of April, we got to know that there was this evacuation program going on, that they were in these camps, there was this intense starvation, and that-- but of course, we didn't know about the gas chambers and that sort of thing.
Because I don't think they were invited-- they didn't go into Belsen.
We went alongside the wires, I told you.

And there on every concrete post with the porcelain insulators for the electrified fence and on each post there was a sign and it was a yellow sign with salmon-colored letters on it which read, [GERMAN].
Well, you know what that means in English?
Entry strongly forbidden, state secret police, Gestapo.
And that's-- I reckon that that would be the first time when you could link it all together.
We knew that gassing went on, but we'd only just learned about it, you know.

We-- after we crossed the Rhine-- we crossed the Rhine on the 23rd of March, '45-- we overran a farm and I found a vehicle backed into a wagon shed and I thought it was a mobile bath unit, the sort of thing you'd be useful to have.
And I said one of my chaps who was fluent in German, do you know, that looks like a mobile bathroom to me.
And I said, we could do with that, you know.
And he went and had look at it and he said, that's not a-- that's-- that's a gas wagon.
And it still had in the box containers, I think it called itself Zyklon gas-- Zyklon B, I think it was called.
But when we looked in the back and saw the filthy mess, there was-- there was no doubt about it what it had been used for, and it wasn't cattle that had been going.
This was after we crossed the Rhine.
We crossed the Rhine, as I told you, 23rd, 24th, 25th, 26th of March.
So this was between the very end of March and the first days in April we saw this lorry.
And I wrote that up in my-- my reminiscences.
Well, I mean, you have to remember we were soldiers.
We realized only too well that the sooner we met the Russians, the better it would be because that would finish it.
But the-- it was just one more thing in a lot of dreadful things which were going on.
And they-- they were dreadful things.
There was millions of people milling around the place, slave labor.
We knew slave labor had been raised all over the continent and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --but I don't think I can really say more than that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It would have been a-- I really don't honestly think one can say more.
It was one of a whole lot of things which put together-- together with the camps which we'd now just seen and the Americans had seen-- the sooner the thing is finished, the better it would be.
And everybody was anxious to get the thing finished, but mind you also anxious to try and stay alive, which is not unreasonable.
Well, we were professional soldiers.
We were not mercenaries, we were professional soldiers, and we fought very hard.
We had Jewish people with us.
But again, I come back to this, they were German Jews.
We had various Jews with us who were Germans who were fighting with us in British uniform with British names to camouflage and protect them in case they were taken prisoner.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can remember two or three of them now, their names.
But I don't know what-- never did know what their Jewish name was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
One was named Lane, he was in an Hungarian Jew and he married-- he married a member of the Rothschild family.
I knew another one who was a German Jew.
In fact, I was with him when he was shot.
His name was Griffiths, his British name, James Griffiths.
But I don't know what his Jewish name was.
After 50 years, that's not bad.
I can still remember the names of two of them.
James Griffiths was killed on the Isle of [INAUDIBLE].
Very good chap.
He lost his boots going across the river and he needed a pair of boots, so he took them a German corpse.
That's using your initiative.
Oh.
Well, he was killed in battle.
It might have been me, it might have been him.
He was killed by a sniper.
Yeah.
He-- just if your name's on it, you get it.
But he was a very good soldier, very good soldier.
Spoke very good English.
I've got a book with a photograph of him in it, actually.
They didn't know.
They didn't know.
It wasn't talked about.
In fact, I didn't know he was a Jew until after he was dead.
But they-- they were brave chaps.
They risked a lot because they were taking up arms against their own race, as it was then the Germans.
And so the British did their best to hide their identity.
I wouldn't know.
I couldn't-- I couldn't answer that question.
All I know that he hated Hitler so much that he was quite prepared to wear a British uniform.
And he really was a very good soldier.
Oh.
Well-- --the kommandos moved on their feet everywhere.
They-- which meant that we went on our feet.
We were not in tanks or anything like that.
They always tried to fight a battle in a manner which they reckoned no one else would tackle so that would give them a surprise.
That in turn meant that we had to think like that.
My job was with the brigade commander and I controlled the artillery.
And that meant my own guns and any others which had been allocated to us.
On the River Rhine, that was well over 800.
And on the Aller, it certainly included medium guns.
And then you chose targets for those guns.
But you always had to be in the right place to do the job.
And I was the brigade commander's gunner, [INAUDIBLE] Roberts, and I knew that the best thing I could do was to stay in his pocket.
And we used to go and he used to run everywhere.
He was very fit.
And we used to have to run, as well.
But we had about 90 pounds on our back in wireless equipment, batteries.
He had nothing except he had a Garand rifle.
Do you now what a Garand rifle is?
It's an automatic rifle.
And he was a deadly shot with it, deadly shot.
He was a good chap.
But I mean, we always went over with the-- either immediately behind the leading wave or with them.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --we worked at night, which meant that from a gunner's point of view, making any reference to targets and that sort of thing was very difficult.

But we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --one of the-- one of the skills which we adopted and we worked very hard at was reading air photographs, and so much so that we would map a course out because we tried to break through and then attack the enemy from the rear.
And we would-- through the air photographs, you can read the sort of land where are were planning to go.
And we were very skilled.
We could say, oh, that's a plowed field or it's grass or it's cultivated.
And you knew as soon as in the dark when he got into that sort of land where you were and you used various methods of staking your time out.
You know, you-- you'd map this route out and then you told the chop who you were working to the route and then you would mark key post-- key points on this route.
And then when you reckoned you were there, you would ask for a round to be thought on, shall we say it was a code named Shark, you would call for Shark one round and they'd fire one round.
And you'd say, well, that should be over there.
And that's how you could keep pace with where you were going.
But it was always exciting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were all supremely confident because we knew that there weren't better gunners anywhere than us and we knew the kommandos were superb at night fighting.
The-- they-- it was an elite force and we all trusted each other.
Well, you'd carry hard rations yourselves.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You would sleep where you were, this is in battle.
You'd get a few minutes whenever you could.
If you had dug in, well, you'd be in a slit trench.
But because of the excitement, the adrenaline rush-- it runs very fast, very fast-- and the-- we would ensure that hot food got to us, particularly in bad weather.
That was the thing which we learned in Scotland when we were training for Arctic warfare, that men who've got empty stomachs get much colder than men who haven't.
And they're not so good soldiers.
We were not averse to eating off the land.
If we came to a German farmer house and there were hausfrau there and we were going to be there for a few minutes, we might tell her to look-- the eggs up and cook them up and we would eat them.
And she would do that.
We would eat their eggs.
Unfortunately, some of the troops used to eat the chickens and then complained there were no eggs.
But we got our ration supplies sent up to us.
The key things a soldier has to have, he has to have his mail from home; he needs food; he needs ammunition; and he needs petrol to get him about.
And of those, food and mail for the individual men is the most important thing.
You've got to get it to him and it's got to be-- in cold weather, it's got to be hot food.
Well, the British Army was very good at that by 1944.
We had tins, like tins of baked beans, that they had a candle in them, a self-heating candle, so you could give you a tin of beans, set her up there-- that's your dinner, eat it up.
And the chap would strike across the top on the pad on the candle and then the candle would burn.
And then with the candle finished burning, he'd open up the can and he had a can of hot baked beans.
Pretty good.
As a matter of fact, I took my wife on a battlefield tour to or three years after the war finished and we went to the Isle of [INAUDIBLE] and I said, now, that's my slit trench.
You could still see the slit trench.
I said, if you ferret down in that corner, you'll find a self-heating soup can which I buried there after I'd eaten the soup.
And she found it.
She was quite pleased.
